Challenges: - Heat, sand, and remoteness. - Security issues. - Moving dunes can bury markers. - Lack of stable landmarks. - Disagreement over datums: a meridian on one datum is not exactly the same as on another, potentially shifting the line by hundreds of meters. Modern tech helps: - Satellite imagery for planning. - GNSS for precise placement. - Digital mapping for shared datasets. Even so, demarcation is political as much as technical; if either side dislikes the outcome, they can contest the interpretation.
You just mentioned datums. Can datum differences really change an international border meaningfully?

They can, especially for boundaries defined by coordinates or meridians/parallels. A geodetic datum defines: - The size/shape of the reference ellipsoid approximating Earth, - Its alignment relative to Earth’s center, - The coordinate reference frame used to express latitude/longitude. Older boundaries might be based on: - Astronomical observations (latitude from star angles; longitude from chronometers), effectively tied to local vertical and local reference. - Early ellipsoids like Clarke 1880, Bessel 1841, etc., used regionally. Modern coordinates often use WGS84 or ITRF realizations. If a treaty says “the 20th meridian east,” but doesn’t specify the datum, you could interpret it in different systems. The difference in the physical location of that meridian line on the ground can be on the order of tens to hundreds of meters (sometimes more), depending on region and the datums involved. For many purposes, that might seem small, but it can matter if: - The border passes near a village, road, well, or mine. - The area is otherwise contested. - The border is used to define resource concessions or security zones. That’s why modern boundary commissions typically adopt an agreed datum and produce precise coordinate lists for boundary pillars.
So why didn’t European treaties have the same ambiguity?

Some European treaties did have ambiguities, but several factors reduced their frequency or impact: 1) Longer history of detailed mapping - Many European states developed national mapping agencies and cadastral surveys earlier, producing detailed topographic maps. - Borders often referenced known local features, towns, or long-established administrative lines. 2) Denser settlement and stronger local institutions - Because borders often ran through populated areas, disputes became salient quickly and were resolved (or at least managed) earlier. - Local land records and jurisdictional practices could anchor the boundary. 3) Different kinds of boundary descriptions - European boundary treaties frequently used “natural” markers (rivers, ridges) and named settlements, which are easier to identify than an abstract coordinate in a poorly mapped region. - Where straight segments existed, they were often short and could be surveyed with relative ease. 4) Earlier demarcation and maintenance - Many European borders were physically marked and maintained over time, with periodic joint commissions to repair markers and update maps. That said, Europe also had—and has—boundary disputes and ambiguities (e.g., shifting river channels, contested maritime boundaries, and micro-territorial anomalies). It’s just that the stereotype of “natural Europe vs. geometric Africa” flattens a much more complex reality.
How much of the “straightness” is just because Africa is large and maps are small-scale?

That’s a significant part of it. At small scale (zoomed out): - Curves and wiggles below a certain size aren’t visible. - A boundary that follows a river with many meanders can look like a smooth arc or even a near-straight line if the river’s overall trend is consistent. - Conversely, a boundary composed of many short straight segments can look like one long straight line. Africa’s size means that on a typical classroom map: - A 50–100 km deviation might be visually minor. - Many boundaries are drawn with generalized cartographic simplification to keep the map readable. But it’s not only a scale illusion. There are truly long straight segments, especially across the Sahara and parts of southern Africa. The key is that both things are true: there’s real geometry, and there’s also a lot of simplification.
What about maritime boundaries? Those are geometric everywhere, right?

Maritime boundaries are often geometric worldwide because they’re frequently based on: - Equidistance/median lines between coasts, - Arcs around islands, - Straight baselines and territorial sea limits measured in nautical miles, - Coordinates defined under the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) framework and related jurisprudence. Even when geography matters (coastline shape, concavity, islands), the final legal boundary is typically expressed as a series of coordinate points connected by geodesic segments. So on a map, it will often look like straight lines or smooth arcs. In Africa, maritime boundary issues include: - Gulf of Guinea oil/gas zones, - The Somalia–Kenya maritime dispute (adjudicated at the ICJ), - Island-related delimitations (e.g., around Equatorial Guinea, São Tomé and Príncipe’s region, etc.). Europe also has many geometric maritime boundaries (North Sea, Mediterranean), so here the “Africa vs Europe” contrast doesn’t really apply.
Going back inland: can you explain the difference between a boundary following a river’s thalweg versus its midline? I see that in disputes.

Yes. For river boundaries, states need a rule to decide where the boundary lies within the river channel. Common doctrines: 1) Thalweg principle - The boundary follows the thalweg: the line of deepest channel, often associated with the main navigable channel. - Rationale: it preserves equal access to navigation for both states when the river is navigable. 2) Midline (median line) principle - The boundary follows the geometric centerline of the river, regardless of depth or navigation channel. - Rationale: it’s simpler in non-navigable rivers or where navigation isn’t a key issue. Complications: - Rivers move (accretion/erosion) and sometimes shift suddenly (avulsion). - If the boundary follows the thalweg, and the channel migrates gradually, the boundary may move with it (accretion). If the river jumps course suddenly (avulsion), the boundary may stay in the old channel, depending on legal interpretation and treaty wording. - Braided rivers have multiple channels; identifying “the” thalweg can be contentious. - Islands: treaties might specify whether islands belong to one side, are split, or follow the thalweg around them. Many disputes boil down to: - What did the original treaty intend? - Is the river navigable? - Which channel counts as the main channel? - How do we treat historical maps vs current hydrology?
Are there African borders that do follow rivers and mountains extensively, contradicting the stereotype?

Absolutely. River-following borders: - DRC–Republic of the Congo follows the Congo River for a significant stretch. - Senegal–Mauritania follows the Senegal River (with historical tensions, but it’s a river boundary). - South Africa–Namibia follows the Orange River (though the exact legal placement has been debated). - Uganda–DRC uses parts of lakes and rivers in the Albertine Rift region. Mountain/watershed borders: - Rwanda–DRC and Uganda–DRC include mountainous segments around the Virunga range. - Morocco–Algeria’s boundary region relates in part to Saharan and Atlas transitions, though the modern border is also shaped by colonial treaties and subsequent state relations. - Ethiopia–Eritrea includes highland areas where ridges and escarpments matter, though demarcation disputes were political and legal rather than purely physical. Also, lakes form boundaries: - Lake Victoria among Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania (though the lake boundary is more complex than many assume). - Lake Tanganyika among DRC, Burundi, Tanzania, Zambia. - Lake Malawi/Nyasa dispute between Malawi and Tanzania hinges on colonial-era interpretations. So the continent contains both types: geometric and physiographic boundaries. The “straightness” is visually salient because the longest uninterrupted straight segments tend to be in Africa’s large arid zones and were drawn in a period of rapid colonial delimitation.
Can you talk about the Lake Malawi/Nyasa dispute? That seems like a case where “natural boundary” still causes disagreement.

Yes—Lake Malawi (also called Lake Nyasa) is a classic example of a “natural feature” not automatically yielding a clear boundary. The core issue: - Malawi’s position historically has been that the boundary runs along the eastern shoreline of the lake—meaning Malawi controls almost the entire lake up to Tanzania’s shore. - Tanzania’s position is that the boundary should run along the median line (or some equitable division), as is common with many lakes between states. Why the disagreement exists: - Colonial-era treaties and administrative arrangements: The boundary was influenced by how the British administered Nyasaland (Malawi) and Tanganyika (Tanzania). Some colonial documents have been interpreted as placing the boundary along the shore. - Post-independence interpretations: Tanzania argues for a more typical international practice of splitting a lake by median line, especially relevant for resource rights (fisheries, potential hydrocarbons). Why “natural” doesn’t settle it: - A lake is a feature, but you still need a rule: shore-to-shore? median line? thalweg analog? historical usage? - Colonial-era language can be ambiguous or reflect administrative convenience rather than modern sovereignty concepts. This demonstrates a broader point: even when borders follow rivers/lakes, disputes can arise because the boundary is a legal construct that must specify how the feature is used as a delimiter.
So the idea that rivers and mountains are “natural borders” is kind of misleading?

It can be misleading if taken to mean “objective and conflict-free.” Rivers and mountains can be convenient boundary markers, but they aren’t inherently “natural” in a political sense. Reasons: - Rivers shift; mountains are complex (multiple ridges, passes, plateaus). - People live, trade, and migrate across them. They can be connectors as much as barriers. - Even if a ridge seems obvious on the ground, translating “the watershed” into a precise line requires decisions about which branch of a divide counts where the topography is complex. What’s “natural” is often retrospective: once a boundary is established, people treat it as obvious and inevitable. But the choice to use a river rather than a parallel is political and administrative, not purely geographic.
If we wanted to quantify “straightness” of borders objectively, how would we do it?

You can quantify border “straightness” using geometric metrics applied to boundary polylines in GIS. Several approaches: 1) Sinuosity / tortuosity index - Compute the ratio: (length of border polyline) / (distance between its endpoints). - A perfectly straight segment has ratio 1. Higher values mean more winding. - Limitation: sensitive to scale and generalization; also depends on how you choose “segments” and endpoints. 2) Fractal dimension / complexity measures - Use box-counting or similar methods to estimate how boundary complexity changes with measurement scale. - More complex boundaries have higher fractal dimension. - Limitation: requires careful methodological choices and can be hard to interpret for political lines. 3) Angular change / curvature statistics - Measure cumulative turning angle along the polyline; straight borders have low cumulative angular change. - Or compute distribution of vertex angles and segment lengths: geometric borders often have long segments with few vertices. 4) Comparison to best-fit line - For a given border section, compute the mean perpendicular deviation from a best-fit straight line. - Useful for identifying “mostly straight” segments even if there are minor bends. 5) “Grid alignment” metric - Measure how often segments align with cardinal directions (N–S, E–W) or at fixed bearings (e.g., 45°). Colonial geometric borders often align with meridians/parallels, though not always. Data considerations: - Use a consistent dataset (e.g., Natural Earth, GADM, official boundary datasets). - Control for map scale and generalization: borders digitized at different resolutions will yield different complexity metrics. You could then compare distributions across continents, but you’d want to account for biome/terrain (desert vs mountains) and historical period of boundary formation to avoid attributing everything to “colonialism” or “Europe vs Africa.”
Interesting. If we ran those metrics, would Africa actually be “straighter” than Europe?

Likely yes in some aggregate sense, but the result would depend strongly on methodology. Why Africa might come out “straighter”: - Presence of very long straight segments in Sahara and parts of southern Africa. - Many boundaries that are meridians/parallels or near-straight lines between points. - Lower prevalence of micro-boundaries tied to centuries of local jurisdictions. Why the result could be nuanced: - Europe has very complex borders in places (Balkans, Central Europe, microstates, historical enclaves). - But Europe also has long “clean” borders where a mountain chain forms a relatively smooth divide (which can be “straight” in a sinuosity sense even if not geometric). - Africa also has very complex borders in the Great Lakes region and West Africa’s coastal mosaic. Also, Europe has a lot more borders per unit area due to many small states, which could skew complexity metrics if not normalized. So: an honest quantitative study might show Africa has a higher proportion of long, low-vertex, grid-aligned segments, but it would also show wide variation within each continent.
Can you explain how “spheres of influence” worked? Were they like vague claims before real borders?

“Spheres of influence” were essentially informal or semi-formal understandings among colonial powers about who had priority to expand, trade, or administer in a region, often before precise borders existed. Characteristics: - Vague spatial extent: described by river basins, hinterlands behind coastal posts, or broad directional claims (“in the hinterland of X”). - Not always matched by effective control on the ground. - Used to avoid direct conflict between European powers while they competed for territory. How they became borders: - As powers established posts, signed treaties with local rulers (often under coercive or unequal circumstances), and conducted military campaigns, spheres were converted into colonies/protectorates with more definite boundaries. - Bilateral treaties then drew delimitation lines to separate these expanding zones. This is where straight lines often enter: - When two spheres met in a poorly mapped interior, negotiators could agree on a meridian/parallel or a straight line connecting known points rather than trying to trace an uncertain watershed. So spheres were like “claims in principle,” and borders were the formalized line once powers needed clarity for administration and to reduce inter-imperial disputes.
If local geography and societies were ignored, why weren’t there more boundary changes after independence?

There were some changes, but many African states largely retained colonial borders due to a combination of political and practical reasons. Key principle: uti possidetis juris - This is a doctrine used in decolonization contexts: newly independent states inherit the administrative boundaries that existed at independence as international borders. - The idea is to prevent widespread territorial disputes and wars that might erupt if every group sought to redraw borders based on ethnicity or historical claims. Institutional support: - The Organization of African Unity (OAU), predecessor to the African Union, generally supported maintaining inherited borders to preserve stability. Practical constraints: - Redrawing borders is extremely contentious: which historical moment do you choose? Which groups get a state? What about mixed areas? - Many populations are intermingled; “ethnic borders” are rarely clean. - Resource distribution, ports, capitals, and infrastructure networks complicate changes. Also, some boundaries were already entrenched in administration: - Tax systems, legal jurisdictions, civil service structures, and international recognition all depended on existing borders. That said, there have been changes: - Eritrea’s independence from Ethiopia. - South Sudan’s independence from Sudan. - Minor adjustments and demarcations between states. But wholesale redrawing has been avoided because the perceived costs of destabilization are high.
Some people say Africa’s borders are “artificial” but Europe’s are “natural.” Is there a better way to phrase it?

A better framing is: - Many African international borders were “externally delimited” in a compressed time frame during colonial partition, often with limited local consultation, and expressed in administrative-geometric terms. - Many European borders were “historically accreted” over long periods through state formation processes, often aligning with physical geography and pre-existing jurisdictions, but still ultimately political and contingent. So instead of “artificial vs natural,” you might say: - “rapidly imposed administrative boundaries” vs “incrementally negotiated historical boundaries” - “coordinate-defined segments” vs “feature-defined segments” - “colonial-era delimitation” vs “long-run consolidation” Because all borders are human decisions. Physical geography can influence where borders end up, but it doesn’t determine them.
Can you give an example where Europe has a very “artificial” border?

A few examples often cited: - The Sykes–Picot Agreement is Middle East, not Europe, but it’s the archetype people think of for artificial lines. Within Europe proper: - Some borders in the Balkans and Eastern Europe were drawn or redrawn in the 20th century to fit political goals, sometimes cutting across mixed populations. - The border between Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland is political and historical; it does not follow a major natural barrier and cuts through local geographies. - The borders around Kaliningrad (Russia) are a result of WWII settlement and population transfers—very political rather than “natural.” - The straight segments of some borders in Central/Eastern Europe reflect treaty lines and administrative decisions. Europe also has “micro-artificiality”: - Enclaves, exclaves, and tiny border anomalies from medieval landholdings and feudal jurisdictions, which are politically artificial even if historically deep. So Europe is not a realm of purely natural borders; it just has fewer extremely long straight segments across sparsely populated terrain.
How do modern GIS datasets decide where a border is when there’s a dispute?

Most global GIS boundary datasets handle disputes by adopting one of these strategies: 1) De facto control - Show the boundary as administered on the ground (who controls it), sometimes with a note. 2) De jure claim - Use the boundary recognized by the dataset’s reference authority (e.g., UN cartographic section, a particular state, or an international body). 3) Depict disputed areas separately - Use dashed lines, hatching, or separate “disputed” polygons. - Provide multiple versions or attributes indicating claims. 4) Neutral representation - Some datasets try to avoid taking a position by omitting a line in disputed areas or using special symbology. The choice is political and can affect analytics. For example: - Calculating area of a country depends on whether disputed territory is included. - Resource or population statistics can shift. High-quality datasets document their policy. For research, it’s good practice to: - State the dataset and version used, - Note any disputes relevant to your analysis, - Consider sensitivity analyses using alternate boundary interpretations.
Are there “straight” borders in Africa that were actually drawn by Africans pre-colonially?

Pre-colonial African polities did have boundaries, but they were often conceptual zones of influence rather than precisely surveyed lines. Where boundaries were recognized, they often followed: - Natural features (rivers, ridges), - Frontier zones (buffer areas), - Control over trade routes and settlements. That said, “straight lines” in the modern cartographic sense are tightly linked to: - Surveying technology, - The legal concept of fixed linear sovereignty boundaries, - Modern state administration. So while you can find cases of relatively linear frontiers (e.g., along a path or between key points), the phenomenon of long, coordinate-defined straight international borders is primarily associated with colonial and modern state practices rather than pre-colonial boundary-making.
Could you talk about the Gambia? Its shape looks like a river corridor—was that also colonial bargaining?

The Gambia is a great example of a border that follows a river corridor rather than a straight line, and yes, it’s strongly shaped by colonial bargaining. Why it’s shaped that way: - The Gambia River was a key navigable route inland from the Atlantic, valuable for trade (historically including the slave trade, later commodities like groundnuts/peanuts). - Britain established control along the river, while France controlled the surrounding territory (Senegal and the broader French West Africa). How the boundary formed: - The British colony/protectorate was essentially defined by control of the river and a narrow band of territory along its banks. - Treaties between Britain and France in the 19th century delimited the extent of British control along the river, often described as a certain distance from the riverbanks, with later adjustments. So the Gambia’s shape is “natural-feature-driven” (river corridor) but still “colonial-artifact” in that it reflects European competition and treaty-making, not an organic nation-state boundary based on local polities. It also illustrates that colonial borders aren’t uniformly straight; they can be shaped around strategic infrastructure like rivers and ports.
That’s interesting because it’s still “artificial” even though it follows a river.

Exactly. “Follows a river” doesn’t mean “non-artificial.” It just means the delimiter chosen was a physical feature. The decision to make that river corridor a separate political unit—rather than part of a larger surrounding state—was political and economic. You can think of it as: - Natural feature as a convenient outline, - Political economy as the reason the outline became a sovereign boundary.
What about Nigeria’s borders? They look irregular and not super straight. How were those formed?

Nigeria’s borders reflect a mix of factors: - Coastal colonial competition: Britain’s consolidation along the Gulf of Guinea coast and inland expansion interacted with French and German (Cameroon) interests. - River systems: The Niger and Benue were major transport arteries and influenced British penetration and administrative organization, though international borders don’t simply trace the rivers. - Protectorates and internal amalgamation: Nigeria was formed by merging different British protectorates/colonies (Northern and Southern Nigeria) in 1914. Internal administrative boundaries were later repurposed or influenced regional identities. - Neighbor boundaries: Nigeria’s borders with Niger, Benin, Cameroon, and Chad include a mixture of straight segments, river/lake segments (Lake Chad), and negotiated lines. The Cameroon border, for example, includes complex segments influenced by German Kamerun and British/French partition after WWI, including the former British Cameroons plebiscite that affected Nigeria/Cameroon boundaries. So Nigeria looks less “ruler-drawn” partly because: - It sits in a region where multiple colonial powers met and negotiated in more detailed ways, - There are more rivers, settlements, and pre-existing political structures that colonial administration interacted with, - Some boundaries reflect post-WWI mandates and later adjustments.
You mentioned Lake Chad. That’s another lake boundary—are there disputes there too?

Lake Chad has been complicated, though the issues are often more about resources, water fluctuation, and security than classic interstate boundary litigation. Challenges: - The lake’s size and shoreline have changed dramatically over decades due to climate variability and water extraction. This affects fishing grounds, grazing areas, and settlement patterns. - The boundary among Chad, Nigeria, Niger, and Cameroon involves both water and land segments. As water recedes, new land emerges—raising questions about jurisdiction and local use. - Even if the legal boundary is defined, administering it in a shifting wetland environment is difficult. There have been boundary commissions and regional cooperation mechanisms (e.g., Lake Chad Basin Commission) that address water management and related issues, which can reduce the salience of precise boundary lines—though security crises can increase it.
If borders are so political, why do we still teach “natural borders” in geography classes?

It’s a simplification that helps introduce the idea that terrain influences human movement and conflict. Natural features can: - Channel movement (passes, river crossings), - Affect defense (mountain ridges), - Provide clear reference points for delimitation. But the concept becomes misleading when it implies inevitability or legitimacy. A better educational approach is: - Teach physical geography as a constraint and opportunity structure, - Teach borders as institutional outcomes shaped by power, technology, and law. In other words: geography matters, but it doesn’t dictate political lines.
Can you describe how a treaty might define a border using a parallel or meridian? What does the language look like?

Treaty language often uses formulations like: - “From the intersection of the X River with the 10th parallel of north latitude, the boundary shall follow said parallel eastward to the 20th meridian of east longitude…” - “Thence in a straight line to point Y, situated at latitude … and longitude …” - “Thence along the meridian passing through point Z…” They may also combine geometry with physical features: - “From point A the boundary shall proceed along the watershed between the basins of the rivers X and Y until it meets the 12th parallel…” - “Thence along the thalweg of the river…” Older treaties sometimes reference maps: - “As shown on the annexed map” (which can be problematic if the map is small-scale or inaccurate). They may reference landmarks: - “To the well of [name],” “to the mountain of [name],” “to the village of [name].” The more the treaty relies on coordinates, the more important it is to specify datum and measurement method—something many older treaties didn’t do, which later forced interpretive work.
How did colonial powers choose which parallel to use? Like why the 22nd parallel for Egypt–Sudan?

Choices like the 22nd parallel often came from administrative and strategic considerations rather than any deep geographic logic. For Egypt–Sudan: - The 22nd parallel was a convenient, simple line that could be defined clearly. - It corresponded to how the British administered the region under the Anglo-Egyptian condominium framework and their strategic priorities along the Nile corridor. - It also related to perceptions of where “Egypt” ended and “Sudan” began in colonial administrative thinking, though local realities were more complex. In general, parallels/meridians might be chosen because: - They create a simple boundary between competing claims. - They align with existing administrative divisions in a colonial bureaucracy. - They avoid giving one side control of a particular river or oasis chain. - They are easy to communicate and reproduce on maps. So the specific latitude is often contingent: it could have been 21°N or 23°N if negotiations had gone differently. Once set, it becomes entrenched.
Are there cases where a straight border was later “corrected” to follow a river once better mapping was available?

Yes, though “corrected” is a loaded term; it implies the river is the “right” boundary. But there are cases where: - A treaty intended to follow a river but mapped it incorrectly, - Or a straight line was a temporary delimitation pending later surveys, - Or demarcation commissions adjusted lines for practicality. Types of adjustments: 1) Clarifying intended features - If a treaty says “the river X,” but there are two candidate rivers, later commissions might decide which was meant. 2) Minor realignments - To keep a village intact on one side, or to align with a road, or to address access to water points. 3) Replacing a geometric line with a feature line - Sometimes states agree to shift from a coordinate line to a watershed or river if it reduces disputes and is mutually acceptable. But many straight borders persist because altering them can reopen broader claims. Even if a river boundary seems more practical, the political cost of renegotiation can be high.
Speaking of rivers moving, doesn’t that make river borders worse than straight lines?

From a purely legal/administrative perspective, river borders can indeed be more complex: - They can shift with accretion. - They can create disputes about islands and channels. - They require ongoing monitoring. Straight-line borders, once demarcated with coordinates and pillars, are stable in a geometric sense. However, river borders can also be advantageous: - They are visible and easily recognized locally. - They can reduce the need for dense pillar networks. - They align with common-sense perceptions (“the river is the boundary”). Whether one is “better” depends on context: - In a shifting delta, a coordinate-defined line might be more stable. - In a forested or mountainous region, a river might be the most practical reference. So the choice is a tradeoff between physical visibility and legal stability.
Can we talk about enclaves and exclaves? Africa has Cabinda, for example. Is that related to colonial mapmaking too?

Yes. Cabinda (an exclave of Angola separated from the rest of Angola by a strip of the Democratic Republic of the Congo along the Congo River’s mouth) is a colonial-era artifact. How it emerged: - Portuguese influence and treaties in the region created a separate status for Cabinda compared to Angola proper. - The Berlin Conference-era recognition of Portuguese claims and subsequent treaties helped formalize the separation. - When Angola became independent, Cabinda was incorporated as part of Angola, creating the modern exclave. Enclaves/exclaves can arise from: - Treaties preserving a coastal foothold, - Control of a port, - Historical protectorate arrangements, - River-mouth strategic considerations. Europe has many micro-enclaves from feudal holdings; Africa has fewer, but those that exist often reflect colonial treaties and coastal strategic logic.
If we wanted to visualize the “colonial geometry,” what map projection issues should we be careful about? Straight lines on a globe aren’t straight on all projections.

Good point. “Straight” depends on what you mean: - A line of constant latitude (a parallel) is not a geodesic except at the equator. On many projections it appears straight (e.g., Mercator shows parallels as straight horizontal lines), but on the globe it’s a small circle. - A line of constant longitude (a meridian) is a geodesic (a great circle half) and will appear straight on many cylindrical projections. - A “straight line” drawn on a flat map between two points might correspond to a rhumb line (constant bearing) on Mercator, not a great-circle route. For political borders: - If a treaty specifies a meridian or parallel, that’s defined on the globe/ellipsoid, not in a projection. - If it says “a straight line from point A to point B,” interpretation can vary: is it a geodesic on the ellipsoid, a rhumb line, or a straight line on a specific map? Modern practice tends to interpret it as a geodesic unless otherwise specified, but historical practice varied. So when visualizing: - Use a projection that doesn’t distort your perception of straightness across the region (an equal-area or conformal projection centered on the region). - Better: visualize on the ellipsoid in GIS and draw geodesics for “straight” segments. - Be explicit: are you showing straight in projected coordinates or geodesic straightness? This is one reason cartographic depictions can mislead: a border defined as a meridian will look straight on many maps, but other “straight” borders might appear curved depending on projection.
Do African borders ever follow lines of longitude/latitude exactly, or are they just approximately aligned?

Some do follow them quite explicitly, at least in legal definition, though on the ground demarcation may introduce small deviations. Examples include: - Egypt–Sudan along 22°N (political boundary claim). - Parts of borders in the Sahara defined by specific meridians/parallels in colonial treaties. - Some southern African boundaries include segments aligned to latitude/longitude. But “exactly” can be tricky because: - Different datums shift the physical location of a given coordinate. - Demarcation markers might be placed with the best available technology at the time; older surveys had errors. - Later mapping may “snap” the line to a coordinate in digital datasets even if the demarcated line is slightly off. So the legal intent may be exact, but operational reality can be approximate unless modern re-demarcation has occurred.
How do you reconcile a border defined by a meridian with local land use? Imagine a pastoral route crossing it—does the straight line cause problems?

It can. A coordinate-defined border can cut across: - Seasonal migration routes, - Shared water points, - Grazing commons, - Kinship networks and markets. In many regions, local communities historically managed mobility through customary arrangements and negotiated access. A hard international border with customs, policing, and differing legal regimes can disrupt that. However, impacts vary: - Some borders remain porous in practice, with informal crossings and local accommodations. - In other cases, security concerns lead to tighter enforcement, making the border more disruptive. States may mitigate issues through: - Cross-border agreements for pastoral mobility, - Local border markets, - Joint management of water points, - Special passes or transhumance corridors. So a straight line is not inherently worse than a river line; either can disrupt mobility if it becomes a hard barrier. The key is governance and cross-border institutional arrangements.
Are there cases where straight borders were chosen specifically to avoid splitting a group, like as a compromise?

Sometimes negotiators used straight lines as compromises precisely because they didn’t want to adjudicate complex local realities—or because they lacked knowledge. That can inadvertently split groups, but in some cases it might also avoid privileging one group’s claim over another’s. However, deliberate “ethnographic boundary drawing” was not consistently practiced, and when it was attempted it faced problems: - Ethnic and linguistic distributions are not sharply bounded; they’re gradients and mosaics. - Colonial ethnography often simplified or misclassified groups. - Political authority structures didn’t always align with language groups. So while there were moments where boundary makers considered “tribal” distributions, straight lines were more often a tool of administrative convenience or inter-imperial compromise than a careful attempt at social alignment.
Let’s go deeper on Europe: why do European borders so often follow watersheds? Is that because of medieval notions of “natural frontiers”?

Watersheds became common boundary references in Europe for a mix of practical and ideological reasons. Practical: - Watersheds can be defensible: controlling the ridge means controlling passes and limiting surprise incursions. - They can be stable: ridgelines don’t shift like rivers. - They can be legible: in mountainous terrain, the divide is often visible and known to locals. Administrative: - Many medieval and early modern polities organized around river valleys; the ridge between valleys could separate jurisdictions. - Ecclesiastical boundaries (dioceses) and feudal domains sometimes used ridges as separators. Ideological: - In the 18th–19th centuries, concepts like “natural frontiers” gained rhetorical power, especially in state-building and nationalism (e.g., debates in France about the Rhine). - But ideology often followed strategic interests: a state might claim a river or mountain as “natural” to justify expansion. So watersheds are common in Europe partly because Europe has many mountain systems where watersheds are prominent, and partly because long-term state competition made defensible barriers attractive.
If watersheds are stable, why not use them more in Africa’s highlands?

They are used in some highland regions, but several factors limited their broader adoption in colonial delimitation: - Timing and priority: many highland areas were contested later or were more difficult to penetrate; early claims often started from coasts and rivers. - Complexity: some highlands have intricate drainage patterns; identifying a single watershed line can require detailed surveys. - Strategic bargaining: colonial powers sometimes prioritized access to routes, lakes, or resources over neat watershed boundaries. - Mixed approaches: a border might follow a watershed for a stretch, then switch to a straight line or a river to connect to a known point. Also, some highland regions were incorporated through conquest or protectorate arrangements that didn’t require precise inter-imperial boundaries until later. So it’s not that watersheds were ignored; rather, they were one tool among many, and in low-relief regions geometric tools dominated.
How do you explain Morocco–Algeria? That border seems political and contested, not purely natural.

Morocco–Algeria is a strong example of how colonial boundaries and postcolonial politics intersect. - The border was shaped significantly by French colonial administration: France controlled Algeria as a colony and had protectorate influence in Morocco. Boundaries were defined through treaties and administrative decisions under French dominance. - The region includes desert and semi-arid zones where straight segments are convenient, but also areas where tribal confederations and trade routes historically mattered. - Post-independence, the border became a site of dispute (notably the Sand War of 1963) and remains politically sensitive, contributing to the long closure of the land border. So even if some segments align with physical geography, the border’s meaning and contestation are political. It’s a reminder that “natural feature” doesn’t guarantee political harmony; history and state relations drive outcomes.
Could you explain why the Algeria–Mali border is so straight? Is it just the Sahara?

The Sahara is a big part of why it’s straight in appearance, but the deeper reason is how French colonial territories were organized. - France controlled Algeria and large parts of West Africa (French Sudan, which became Mali; Niger; etc.). - Many of the lines that are now international borders were originally internal administrative boundaries within the French empire—lines separating military or administrative districts. - In the desert, where settlement was sparse and the priority was administrative manageability rather than aligning with local socio-political boundaries, straight lines were efficient. When these colonies became independent states, those internal lines were “internationalized” under uti possidetis. What was once a line between colonial administrative units became a sovereign border. So it’s both environment (desert) and institutional history (French administrative mapping).
That raises a question: are the straight borders more common in former French colonies than British ones?

There are patterns, but it’s not a simple French-versus-British dichotomy. French colonial administration: - Often emphasized centralized administrative units and sometimes used geometric delimitation in the Sahara and Sahel. - The vastness of French West Africa and French Equatorial Africa encouraged large-scale administrative mapping. British colonial approach: - Often used indirect rule and protectorates; boundaries could be shaped by treaties with other powers and by existing local polities. - But British-influenced boundaries also include many straight segments (e.g., parts of Sudan’s borders, Kenya-Uganda segments, etc.). Portuguese and German colonial legacies: - Also contributed to geometric lines and corridor shapes (Caprivi). The strongest predictor of straightness is often: - Biome/terrain (desert/steppe), - Period and speed of delimitation, - Whether the border was primarily an inter-imperial compromise in a poorly mapped area. Colonial “style” matters, but geography and diplomatic context matter at least as much.
If you had to pick the single most “ruler-drawn” border in Africa, what would it be?

It’s hard to pick one because many have long straight stretches. But candidates often cited include: - Egypt–Sudan at 22°N (as a simple parallel, plus the Hala’ib/Bir Tawil anomalies). - Long segments of Libya’s southern borders (Libya–Chad, Libya–Niger, Libya–Sudan) across the Sahara. - Algeria–Mali/Niger segments. If you mean “visually striking on a map,” the 22nd parallel case stands out because it’s a clean latitude line and it produced a very clear set of anomalies (Hala’ib Triangle and Bir Tawil) that make the geometry obvious.
Can you explain Bir Tawil and why it’s “unclaimed” sometimes?

Bir Tawil is a small area between Egypt and Sudan that is sometimes described as “terra nullius” (unclaimed land), though “unclaimed” is a bit of a simplification. Here’s the geometry: - There are two different boundary lines: 1) The 1899 political boundary set at the 22nd parallel (Egypt north, Sudan south). 2) A 1902 administrative boundary adjustment that put the Hala’ib Triangle under Sudanese administration and Bir Tawil under Egyptian administration, for administrative convenience based on tribal land use and access. After independence: - Egypt generally claims the 1899 line (22nd parallel) because that gives it the Hala’ib Triangle on the Red Sea coast. - Sudan generally claims the 1902 administrative line because that also gives it the Hala’ib Triangle. But those claims are mutually exclusive: - If Egypt claims Hala’ib via the 1899 line, it implies Bir Tawil would fall south of that line and thus be Sudanese—yet Sudan’s preferred line would place Bir Tawil in Egypt. - If Sudan claims Hala’ib via the 1902 line, Bir Tawil would be Egyptian. So neither state wants to claim Bir Tawil formally because doing so would undermine its preferred claim to Hala’ib. That’s why Bir Tawil is often described as “unclaimed,” though in practice the situation involves contested sovereignty logic rather than a genuine absence of interest.
That’s a perfect example of administrative vs political boundaries. Does Europe have anything similar?

Europe has some analogous situations—though not identical—where: - Administrative boundaries didn’t match political boundaries, - Or where historical treaties created odd pockets. Examples: - The Neutral Moresnet area (19th century) was a small neutral territory between Prussia and the Netherlands/Belgium due to a zinc mine dispute. It was a kind of “in-between” created by treaty ambiguity. - Various condominium arrangements historically existed (e.g., Andorra historically had shared sovereignty aspects, though it’s now a co-principality with clear status). - Micro-disputes over river islands or small land parcels exist (e.g., along the Danube historically). But the Bir Tawil situation is unusually clean in its “either/or” geometry and the way two boundary definitions create a gap that neither side wants to claim because of the larger prize.
I want to avoid oversimplifying. What are the main caveats when comparing African and European borders?

Main caveats: 1) Scale and perception - Africa is larger; small-scale maps exaggerate straightness and hide complexity. 2) Selection bias - People notice the Sahara’s long straight lines and generalize to the whole continent. 3) Diversity within continents - Africa contains very complex borders (Great Lakes, West African coast) and very geometric ones (Sahara). - Europe contains natural-feature borders and very political/treaty-defined ones. 4) Different historical timelines - Europe’s borders reflect centuries of state formation; Africa’s international borders largely crystallized in a few decades under colonial partition, then were stabilized at independence. 5) Physical geography differences - Europe has many mountain chains and dense river networks in smaller spaces; Africa has vast deserts and plateaus where continuous linear barriers are less prominent. 6) Legal and technical evolution - Modern boundary law and surveying standards were different in different periods, affecting how lines were defined. If you keep those in mind, the comparison becomes less about “Africa is artificial” and more about “different processes, different constraints, different outcomes.”
Suppose someone says, “Straight borders prove colonialism ignored Africans.” How would you respond without denying colonial violence?

You can acknowledge the violence and dispossession of colonialism while still being precise about what border geometry does and doesn’t prove. A careful response might be: - Straight borders are evidence of an administrative-diplomatic process among colonial powers that prioritized inter-imperial agreements and administrative convenience, often with limited local consultation and frequently with coercion. - But straightness alone doesn’t measure the degree of local impact; a river boundary can be equally disruptive, and some straight borders run through sparsely populated areas where disruption was different in kind. - The deeper issue is not the geometry per se, but the external imposition of sovereignty frameworks, the extraction-oriented political economy, and the reorganization of governance. So: border geometry is a clue about the process, but it’s not a complete proxy for colonialism’s harms. That keeps the critique grounded without reducing history to a shape on a map.
That’s helpful. Can you recommend a way to study this topic systematically without falling into clichés?

A systematic approach: 1) Use a mixed-methods design - Quantitative: compute border geometry metrics (sinuosity, vertex density, grid alignment) by region. - Qualitative: pick case studies and read treaty texts, boundary commission reports, and historical maps. 2) Control for environment - Compare deserts to deserts, mountains to mountains, across continents. 3) Distinguish delimitation vs demarcation vs administration - Many clichés conflate these. 4) Incorporate local histories - Look at how borders affected trade networks, pastoralism, taxation, and conflict. 5) Track changes over time - Compare colonial-era maps with post-independence demarcation and modern GIS. 6) Use critical cartography - Analyze how map projections, generalization, and symbology shape perceptions. Sources you might look for (types, not specific titles): - Boundary commission archives, - Colonial treaty collections, - National mapping agency documents, - Scholarly work on African state formation and borderlands, - GIS datasets with dispute annotations. If you tell me a particular region you’re interested in (Sahel, Great Lakes, Horn of Africa), I can propose a structured reading and mapping plan.
Let’s do the Horn of Africa. Borders there look less straight, but there are still disputes. How does that fit the story?

The Horn of Africa is a region where: - Colonial competition involved multiple powers (Britain, Italy, France) plus an existing sovereign empire (Ethiopia) that resisted colonization and negotiated borders. - Terrain is varied: highlands, escarpments, arid lowlands, and strategic coastal zones. - Ethnolinguistic and pastoral mobility patterns cross territories. So you get borders that are: - Less dominated by long straight segments than the Sahara, - More shaped by a mix of treaties, wars, protectorate boundaries, and negotiations with Ethiopia, - Highly contentious in places because of strategic ports, identity politics, and historical claims. Examples: - Eritrea–Ethiopia: colonial Italian Eritrea vs Ethiopian sovereignty; demarcation disputes became a major war. - Somalia’s borders: colonial partition among British Somaliland, Italian Somaliland, French Somaliland (Djibouti), and neighboring territories under Ethiopia and Kenya. - Djibouti’s small territory reflects French strategic interest in a port near the Red Sea route. So the Horn shows that “not straight” doesn’t mean “not colonial,” and “not colonial” (Ethiopia’s case) doesn’t mean “conflict-free.” The drivers include strategic corridors, ports, and postcolonial state politics.
You said Ethiopia negotiated borders as a sovereign state. Did that lead to more “natural” borders?

Not necessarily. Ethiopia’s borders were shaped by: - Treaties with colonial powers (Italy, Britain, France), - Military outcomes, - Strategic considerations (access to the sea historically, control of highlands), - Local power relations. Some segments align with watersheds or natural features, especially in highland areas, but others are more political. Also, even when a border aligns with a ridge, the choice of which ridge line to follow can be contested. So sovereignty in negotiation can change the process (more local agency), but it doesn’t guarantee “natural” geometry. Borders remain political compromises under constraints.
This has been a lot about land borders. How do internal administrative boundaries relate to international ones in Africa?

Internal boundaries matter because many international borders started as internal lines within colonial empires, and after independence many states kept colonial administrative divisions or adapted them. Relationships: - International borders: define sovereignty between states. - Internal boundaries: define provinces/regions/districts within a state. In colonial contexts: - Internal lines were drawn for governance efficiency, taxation, labor recruitment, and control, not necessarily to reflect local identities. - Some internal boundaries later became international when colonies were split (e.g., mandate partitions, protectorate separations). Post-independence: - States sometimes redraw internal boundaries to manage ethnic politics, decentralization, or resource distribution. - Internal boundary changes can be politically sensitive and sometimes more contentious than international boundaries because they affect representation and budgets. The key point: the same administrative logic that produced straight internal lines can, through decolonization, produce straight international borders.
If someone wanted to argue that straight borders are “bad,” is there any evidence that border geometry correlates with instability?

Empirical evidence is mixed and the causal chain is hard to establish. Potential mechanisms: - Straight borders may be more likely to cut across social networks if they ignore local patterns—though river borders can also do that. - Poorly demarcated straight borders in remote areas can create ambiguity and disputes. - Conversely, straight borders can be clearer and reduce disputes if well demarcated. Confounders: - Many straight borders occur in deserts with low population density, which might reduce conflict likelihood. - Instability correlates strongly with governance, economic shocks, external intervention, and civil-military dynamics—factors not reducible to border shape. Some research suggests: - Partitioning of ethnic groups across borders can correlate with conflict risk, but that’s about social geography and state policy, not straightness per se. So if making a rigorous argument, it’s better to focus on: - How borders intersect with settlement and mobility patterns, - The quality of border governance and cross-border institutions, - The presence of valuable resources in frontier zones, rather than using straightness as a proxy for “badness.”
Thanks. I think I have enough. But I want one more: can you summarize the main reasons in a way that directly answers the original question without moralizing?

Africa’s borders often look straighter than Europe’s because many were delimited rapidly during colonial partition using simple geometric rules (parallels, meridians, straight segments) that were easy to specify in treaties and feasible to demarcate in poorly mapped or low-relief regions like deserts. Europe’s borders more often reflect centuries of incremental state formation and repeated treaty adjustments in densely settled, well-mapped landscapes where rivers, ridges, and existing jurisdictions provided convenient reference lines—though Europe also has geometric borders and Africa also has many river- and mountain-following borders. The visual contrast is amplified by map scale and projection, and by the prominence of very long straight segments across the Sahara.
What will the next supercontinent look like? Is Pangaea Ultima the leading theory?

Several competing hypotheses exist for the next supercontinent, and “Pangaea Ultima” (also called “Pangaea Proxima” in some older popularizations, though those labels are sometimes used inconsistently) is one of the more widely discussed endmember scenarios—but it’s not uniquely “the leading” theory in the sense of a single consensus outcome. The main families of scenarios are usually grouped by how the current oceans evolve, especially the Atlantic and Pacific: 1) Introversion: A supercontinent forms by closing the ocean that opened during the previous supercontinent breakup. In this framing, because Pangaea’s breakup created the Atlantic, the next supercontinent forms when the Atlantic closes. “Pangaea Ultima” is commonly presented as an introversion-style scenario. 2) Extroversion: A supercontinent forms by closing the external ocean that surrounded the previous supercontinent. Since Pangaea was surrounded by the Panthalassa ocean, the modern analog is often taken as the Pacific. In extroversion, the Pacific closes and the Americas collide with Asia-Australia. 3) Orthoversion: The next supercontinent forms roughly 90 degrees away from the previous one, influenced by long-lived downwelling zones and subduction “girdles” in the mantle. This yields a different geometry; some versions cluster around the Arctic region. Named proposals you’ll see include: - Amasia: Often an orthoversion-style idea where continents converge near the North Pole, with the Arctic Ocean closing. - Novopangaea: A common extroversion-esque scenario where the Pacific closes; the Atlantic continues to widen. - Pangaea Ultima: Usually introversion; the Atlantic closes; the Americas swing back toward Eurasia/Africa. All are plausible at the level of plate-tectonic mechanics, but long-range predictions (250 million years) are sensitive to subduction initiation/cessation, changes in plate boundary forces, plume events, and mantle convection patterns. The best way to think about it is: we can outline credible trajectories and constraints, not a single deterministic map. If you want, I can walk through the mechanics of each scenario and what would have to happen (e.g., where new subduction zones would need to form in the Atlantic, or how Pacific subduction would have to persist) and then sketch what the continents “look like” in each.
Let’s start with the basics: what controls whether the Atlantic closes or keeps opening? It seems like it’s been opening for a long time.

The Atlantic’s fate hinges on whether it remains a passive-margin ocean (spreading at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, little/no subduction around its margins) or whether subduction initiates along one or more Atlantic margins and begins consuming Atlantic lithosphere. Key controls include: 1) Presence or absence of subduction zones - Right now, most of the Atlantic margins are passive (no major trenches). The big exception is at the eastern edge of the Caribbean (Lesser Antilles) and the Scotia/South Sandwich system, but those are limited and don’t consume the main Atlantic basin in a way that would reverse Atlantic spreading. - For the Atlantic to close, you’d typically need subduction to initiate along the Americas’ east coast, or along the west coasts of Europe/Africa, or in the South Atlantic margins. Subduction initiation is hard; it requires a suitable stress regime and often some pre-existing weakness. 2) Ridge push vs slab pull balance - Plate motions are strongly driven by slab pull: dense, cold oceanic lithosphere sinking at subduction zones. Ridge push is weaker but still matters. - The Atlantic has relatively little slab pull directly attached to Atlantic plates because there are few trenches around it. The plates bordering the Atlantic (North American, South American, African, Eurasian) get slab-pull forces from subduction zones elsewhere (e.g., around the Pacific “Ring of Fire” and the Mediterranean/Indonesia regions). 3) Changes in far-field boundary forces - Even if the Atlantic itself has no trenches, subduction in the Pacific can “pull” plates such that the Atlantic continues to open as a consequence of global plate circuit geometry. - If Pacific subduction reorganizes—say, a major subduction zone shuts down or new subduction begins—plate motions can reorient. Over tens to hundreds of Myr, that can transform an opening ocean into a closing one. 4) Mantle convection and dynamic topography - Large-scale mantle flow can influence plate velocities and stress fields, potentially favoring subduction initiation in some regions. - Long-lived downwellings (e.g., near the edges of large low-shear-velocity provinces, LLSVPs, in the deep mantle) can stabilize certain subduction “girdles,” influencing where oceans tend to close. 5) Buoyancy and age of oceanic lithosphere - Older oceanic lithosphere is denser and more likely to subduct. The Atlantic has some older seafloor but much is relatively young compared to parts of the Pacific. Still, by 250 Myr, Atlantic lithosphere could become old enough that if a trigger occurs, it could subduct readily. So: the Atlantic has been opening since the Jurassic, but that doesn’t guarantee it will keep opening indefinitely. Oceans can flip from passive to active margins, but the “switch” often requires a tectonic trigger—collision, changes in plate motion, transform fault reactivation, plume-related weakening, or compression from far-field forces.
You mentioned subduction initiation is hard. What would actually trigger a new Atlantic subduction zone? Are there examples in Earth history?

Subduction initiation is one of the more debated problems in tectonics because it involves overcoming the strength and buoyancy of relatively young oceanic lithosphere. But there are plausible triggers and some inferred examples. Mechanisms proposed for subduction initiation: 1) Compression across an old transform or fracture zone - Old transforms can be mechanical weaknesses. If plate motions change so that a transform becomes convergent (transpression), the lithosphere can start to buckle and one side can begin to sink. - This is often invoked for “spontaneous” subduction initiation along pre-existing weaknesses, though “spontaneous” is a bit of a misnomer—there’s still a stress driver. 2) Forced subduction initiation from nearby collision - A continental collision can transmit compressive stresses into adjacent oceanic lithosphere, causing it to fail and start subducting. - Example: In some reconstructions, subduction initiation in the western Mediterranean is tied to Africa-Europe convergence and microplate interactions. 3) Subduction polarity reversal and back-arc collapse - If an existing subduction zone retreats or reorganizes, back-arc basins can close and new trenches can form with opposite polarity. - This is common in complex convergent margin systems (western Pacific, Mediterranean). It’s less directly applicable to the Atlantic today, but could occur if the Caribbean or Scotia systems propagate. 4) Plume-related weakening and lithospheric delamination - Large igneous provinces and plume impacts can thermally weaken lithosphere, change buoyancy, and localize deformation. - A plume head near a margin could create rifting, but in a compressive setting it could also facilitate foundering/delamination that helps initiate subduction nearby. 5) Subduction zone “infection” or lateral propagation - Existing subduction can propagate along a margin, converting passive to active over time. This might be the most plausible for the Atlantic: subduction currently exists in the Caribbean and Scotia regions; in principle, those systems could migrate/propagate along the Atlantic margins. Examples (inferred or debated): - Izu-Bonin-Mariana (IBM) subduction initiation (~50 Ma) is one of the best-studied cases. It likely involved reorganization of plate motions and pre-existing weaknesses, leading to rapid initiation and forearc basalt formation. This is in an oceanic setting, not at a passive margin, but it shows subduction can start “new” in geologic terms. - The Puysegur trench (south of New Zealand) is sometimes cited as a relatively young subduction system initiated along an older transform boundary as plate motions changed. - Some interpretations suggest subduction initiation along the Lesser Antilles and Scotia arcs involved complex changes in plate motion and inherited structures. For the Atlantic, speculative triggers might include: - A change in Africa-Eurasia convergence that compresses the eastern Atlantic margins. - A shift in North American plate motion that turns the eastern seaboard into a convergent boundary (perhaps via changes in Pacific subduction geometry). - Propagation of subduction from the Caribbean along the North American east margin (though that’s a big leap and would require substantial reorganization).
If the Pacific is closing now, why isn’t that automatically the “winner” for what closes next? Wouldn’t extroversion be the default?

It’s tempting to treat the current Pacific as “obviously” the ocean that will vanish next because it’s ringed by subduction zones and is shrinking in many sectors. But “default” is tricky because: 1) The Pacific is not uniformly closing - In the eastern Pacific, the East Pacific Rise is a fast-spreading ridge, and subduction consumes oceanic lithosphere along the Americas. In the western Pacific, there’s a complex mix of subduction zones, back-arc spreading, and microplates. Some areas are shrinking, others are being reconfigured. - Net area change depends on the balance of spreading vs subduction. In broad terms, the Pacific basin has been shrinking since at least the Mesozoic, but the geometry of its closure can change. 2) Subduction zones can shut down - If buoyant features (oceanic plateaus, arcs, continents) arrive at trenches, they can jam subduction. Collision can terminate a subduction zone and force reorganization. - Over 250 Myr, it’s plausible that multiple trenches in the Pacific will be interrupted by collisions (e.g., Australia with SE Asia, or eventually the Americas with Asia), but those collisions can also create new subduction elsewhere rather than simply “ending closure.” 3) Plate boundary reorganization is common on tens of Myr timescales - The present arrangement is not stable in the long term. Ridge-trench interactions, slab rollback, and changes in relative plate motions can restructure boundaries. A closing ocean can persist, but the identity of the “main closing basin” can shift. 4) Orthoversion adds a different constraint - Some researchers argue that supercontinents form preferentially over long-lived mantle downwellings, and that the location of those downwellings relative to the last supercontinent leads to a roughly 90° shift. In that view, “what is closing fastest now” is less determinative than “where are the stable subduction girdles and mantle convection patterns.” So extroversion (Pacific closure) is certainly plausible and in some models looks “natural” given current subduction dominance. But it’s not guaranteed because the Pacific system is also the most dynamic and prone to reorganizations. Meanwhile, the Atlantic, despite being passive, could in principle flip if new subduction starts. The uncertainty is largely about whether that flip happens.
Can you give a concrete picture of Novopangaea? Like, what collides with what, and where would the mountain belts be?

In a Novopangaea-style scenario (extroversion-ish), the core idea is: the Pacific closes while the Atlantic continues to widen. That yields a supercontinent assembled largely by bringing the Americas into collision with Asia and Australia. A concrete sequence (one plausible narrative among many): 1) Continued subduction consumes the Pacific - The Americas keep moving westward relative to the mantle frame (details depend on reference frame), with oceanic plates subducting along their western margins. - The western Pacific subduction zones (Japan, Mariana, Tonga-Kermadec, etc.) remain active, though they may migrate via rollback. 2) Australia continues northward and collides more fully with SE Asia - Australia is already moving north. Over tens of Myr, it would further collide with Indonesia and SE Asia, thickening crust and building large mountain belts. - This could resemble an enlarged Himalayan-Tibetan-type orogen but distributed across a broader zone of microcontinents and arcs. 3) The Americas approach East Asia - North America’s west margin and Asia’s east margin are both active convergent systems with arcs and accretionary complexes. - As the Pacific narrows, island arcs and microplates get squeezed, accreted, and uplifted. 4) Major collision: western North America with eastern Asia - When true continent-continent collision occurs, you’d expect: - A massive orogenic belt roughly where the Pacific used to be, possibly spanning from what is now Alaska down through former western North America into a collisional zone with NE Asia. - Similar to the Appalachians (a Pangaea-assembly belt) in scale, but potentially more complex due to the number of arcs and terranes. 5) South America’s role - South America could collide with parts of Antarctica and Australia or with SE Asia depending on the geometry, creating southern collisional belts. - The Drake Passage and Southern Ocean circulation would be disrupted; Antarctica might become more integrated into the supercontinent. 6) The Atlantic keeps opening - If the Atlantic continues to widen, Europe and Africa drift farther from the Americas, possibly forming a large internal seaway or maintaining a broad ocean on the opposite side of the supercontinent. Mountain belts (orogens) you’d expect: - A “Pacific suturing belt” where the Pacific closes: the dominant global mountain chain in this scenario, analogous to the Appalachian-Caledonian-Hercynian belts formed during Pangaea assembly but potentially even longer. - Enhanced orogeny in SE Asia-Australia collision zone: thickened crust, high plateaus, complex metamorphic belts. - Potential reactivation and uplift in other intraplate regions due to far-field stresses; older orogens can be rejuvenated. The exact placement is sensitive to where the final suture runs: does North America hit Siberia? Does it hit China? Does it wrap around toward India? Over 250 Myr, those distinctions matter, but the “headline” is: the Pacific becomes the main collision zone, not the Atlantic.
How does Amasia differ? I’ve seen maps where everything piles up around the Arctic. What has to happen to get that?

Amasia is typically framed as an orthoversion outcome: continents converge near the Arctic (or broadly the high northern latitudes), producing a supercontinent centered near the present-day North Pole. For Amasia to happen, several things are usually implied: 1) The Arctic Ocean closes - Today the Arctic has spreading centers (e.g., the Gakkel Ridge) and complex boundaries. For closure, you’d need subduction to develop and/or for existing convergent systems to migrate. - Alternatively, the Arctic could become a “trap” where surrounding plates converge due to global plate circuit changes. 2) Northward drift of major continents - North America and Eurasia are already largely in the Northern Hemisphere. Africa is moving north, and Australia is moving north. South America’s path is more variable but could also trend northward depending on boundary reorganizations. 3) Persistence or development of subduction zones that steer plates poleward - Plate motions often align with slab-pull vectors. If major subduction zones develop around the Arctic perimeter (or if existing ones reorganize), they could pull plates toward the pole. 4) The Atlantic and Pacific don’t simply close “in place” - In orthoversion, neither of the major oceans necessarily closes completely as the primary route. Instead, closure happens along a belt orthogonal to the previous supercontinent’s center. - Some versions have the Atlantic continuing to open while the Pacific continues to close, but the final assembly happens in the Arctic as the remaining gaps close there. 5) Mantle convection constraint (the “90-degree rule”) - The orthoversion concept suggests that the location of the next supercontinent is influenced by the pattern of subduction-driven downwellings that formed after the last supercontinent broke apart. - The idea is that subduction zones tend to organize into a girdle around the planet, and the next supercontinent forms over those downwellings; because that girdle is often ~90° from the old supercontinent’s center, you get a quarter-turn in supercontinent location. In map terms, Amasia often shows: - Eurasia and North America colliding across the Arctic. - Africa and possibly South America and Australia migrating north and docking onto the assemblage. - Antarctica may remain isolated longer or be dragged north depending on the details. The big difference from Novopangaea is the “where” of the final sutures: Amasia’s key sutures are in the Arctic realm rather than across the equatorial Pacific.
Okay, now the one I originally asked about: Pangaea Ultima. What does that look like in a more detailed step-by-step?

Pangaea Ultima (introversion) is usually described as: the Atlantic closes, bringing the Americas back into collision with Europe/Africa, roughly reassembling a Pangaea-like configuration but not identical. A plausible step-by-step narrative: 1) Atlantic spreading slows and new subduction begins - The Mid-Atlantic Ridge either slows or becomes less dominant as a plate boundary force. - Subduction initiates somewhere along the Atlantic margins. Many popular depictions place new trenches along the eastern coast of the Americas (Atlantic-facing side), though alternative versions put them along western Europe/Africa. - Initiation could be segmented—multiple trenches starting in the South Atlantic, then propagating. 2) The Americas’ motion changes - With a new Atlantic trench, the Americas could begin moving eastward relative to Europe/Africa (or Europe/Africa could move westward), progressively narrowing the Atlantic. - The Caribbean region likely becomes a major tectonic knot, with microplates squeezed and reorganized. 3) Closure of the Atlantic - As the Atlantic narrows, passive margins become active convergent margins. Thick sediment wedges get scraped off into accretionary prisms; foreland basins develop on the continents. - A new “Atlantic orogen” forms—an Appalachian-scale or larger mountain chain—running roughly north-south where the Atlantic used to be. 4) Pacific evolution during this time - The Pacific might continue closing too, but if the Atlantic becomes the dominant closing basin, the Pacific could be reduced, reorganized, or even partially re-opened in some areas depending on ridge-trench interactions. - Some Pangaea Ultima maps show the Pacific becoming a smaller internal ocean or a remnant basin. 5) Africa continues north; Mediterranean closes early - On shorter timescales, Africa’s northward motion closes the Mediterranean, creating an Alpine-Himalayan-style orogen in southern Europe. - In the longer term, Africa’s position in the final supercontinent depends on whether it becomes central (like in Pangaea) or shifts due to rotations. 6) Final assembly - North America collides with Europe (Greenland-Scandinavia/UK/Iberia region) and/or with northwest Africa depending on rotations. - South America collides with Africa, building a southern continuation of the Atlantic orogen. - Antarctica and Australia could end up attached along the southern margins, but the exact docking is variable. What the map “looks like”: - A single giant landmass roughly straddling the equator, with a large interior arid zone. - A major mountain belt along the closed Atlantic suture, perhaps comparable in length to the old Appalachian-Variscan system but potentially higher during active collision. - Remnant oceans: Depending on the variant, you might get a reduced Pacific basin or a new “internal sea” where parts of the old oceans remain trapped. The key requirement is the big one: subduction must start in the Atlantic and outcompete continued spreading. That’s why some geoscientists treat Pangaea Ultima as a useful scenario but not necessarily the most likely.
When people draw these future maps, what are they actually basing them on? Is it just extrapolating GPS velocities?

It’s partly extrapolation, but serious attempts use a mix of kinematic plate reconstructions, constraints from mantle dynamics, and analogs from past cycles. Still, the uncertainty grows rapidly with time. Main ingredients: 1) Current plate motions (GPS + geologic plate circuits) - GPS provides present-day velocities (mm/yr) for plates and some microplates. - Plate motion models (like NUVEL-type or more modern ones) integrate seafloor spreading rates, transform azimuths, earthquake slip vectors, and hotspot tracks. - Extrapolating these vectors linearly for 250 Myr is not realistic, but it provides a starting direction. 2) Known boundary conditions and “near-future” inevitabilities - Some events are highly likely on ~10–50 Myr timescales: - Africa continuing to converge with Europe. - Australia continuing north into SE Asia. - East African Rift continuing to evolve (whether it becomes a new ocean depends on rift success). - These near-term constraints set initial conditions for longer-term evolution. 3) Plate boundary physics and statistical tendencies - Oceans tend to close where subduction already exists. - Passive margins tend to persist, but they can become active. - Ridge-trench interactions can eliminate spreading centers and reorganize plates. 4) Paleogeographic analogs - The supercontinent cycle provides templates: Rodinia breakup and reassembly, Pangaea assembly, etc. - But analogs are imperfect because mantle structure and continental configurations differ. 5) Mantle convection modeling - Some studies couple plate motion to mantle flow, using seismic tomography to infer present-day slabs and deep mantle structures. - Forward models can explore how slabs sink, how plumes rise, and how that might reorganize plate boundaries. - These models are sensitive to rheology assumptions, viscosity structure, and how plates are coupled to the mantle. 6) Scenario-building (not prediction) - Many “future supercontinent” maps are best interpreted as scenario sketches: if X happens (e.g., Atlantic subduction initiates), then Y follows. - They’re not probabilistic forecasts unless explicitly framed that way. So: it’s not just GPS extrapolation, but it’s also not a precise forecast. It’s constrained imagination with physics and precedent.
Let’s get technical: what are the timescales? If the Atlantic started subducting tomorrow, how long to close it?

A rough calculation can be made from present basin widths and plausible convergence rates, but it depends strongly on where subduction initiates and how fast plates move. Ballpark numbers: - The Atlantic is widest in the North Atlantic at several thousand kilometers (order 5,000–7,000 km depending on where you measure). - Typical plate convergence/subduction rates can be ~2–10 cm/yr (20–100 km/Myr), with fast systems on the high end (e.g., parts of the Pacific) and slower on the low end. If you imagine a sustained average convergence of, say, 5 cm/yr (50 km/Myr): - 6,000 km / (50 km/Myr) ≈ 120 Myr to close. At 2 cm/yr: - 6,000 km / (20 km/Myr) ≈ 300 Myr. At 8 cm/yr: - 6,000 km / (80 km/Myr) ≈ 75 Myr. But “started subducting tomorrow” is not just turning on a conveyor belt: - Subduction initiation could be slow and segmented; early convergence might be modest. - As slabs grow, slab pull can accelerate convergence, but collisions and buoyant features can slow it later. - Closure is not uniform: the South Atlantic could close earlier or later than the North Atlantic depending on geometry. So closure within ~100–200 Myr is plausible if a robust subduction system develops and persists. That fits the 250 Myr horizon, but it’s conditional.
You keep mentioning mantle structures like LLSVPs. How do those actually influence where continents go? Continents are on the surface; why does deep mantle matter?

Deep mantle structure matters because it influences where hot upwellings (plumes) and cold downwellings (subducted slabs) tend to organize, and those in turn affect plate boundary forces and the long-term pattern of convection that plates ride on. Key links: 1) Slabs drive convection and create long-lived downwellings - Subducting plates sink through the mantle. Over tens to hundreds of Myr, the accumulation of slabs can form persistent downwelling regions. - Downwellings are associated with mantle flow that can “anchor” subduction zones in broad regions, making some belts of subduction more stable over geologic time. 2) LLSVPs and plume generation zones - LLSVPs (Large Low Shear Velocity Provinces) are deep mantle regions (near the core-mantle boundary) with anomalously low seismic velocities, often interpreted as hotter and/or compositionally distinct. - The edges of LLSVPs are thought to be favorable locations for plume generation. That can influence where large igneous provinces erupt at the surface, which can affect rifting and plate boundary changes. 3) Dynamic topography and basal tractions - Mantle flow exerts tractions on the base of plates, influencing stress fields and potentially steering plate motions over long timescales. - Dynamic topography (surface uplift/subsidence caused by mantle flow) can affect erosion, sedimentation, and the loading of margins—secondary effects that can influence tectonics. 4) Supercontinent insulation and mantle reorganization - When a supercontinent forms, it can “insulate” the mantle beneath, potentially altering convection patterns and plume activity. That can contribute to breakup later. - The deep mantle may retain memory of past subduction patterns, influencing where the next assembly occurs (a core idea in orthoversion). So deep mantle isn’t “controlling continents like magnets,” but it shapes the convective framework and the distribution of forces that make some tectonic configurations more likely than others.
